metadata.scheduling_note: All ingredients in this MVP library are unscheduled. The S2/S3/S4 practitioner scope filter has no effect at this stage and will be activated in a future revision when scheduling status is added per ingredient.

ingredients.label_expression:
Panax quinquefolius (American ginseng) root extract
equivalent Panax quinquefolius dry root
equivalent ginsenosides
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 1-3 g/day dried herb equivalent https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10761967/ happy with this TR 400 - 750 mg of a 4:1 extract

clinical_details.introduction: Ginseng root has been extensively used as a stimulant and a tonic to preserve health, improve energy and vitality. It may stimulate brain function and has also been shown to improve blood sugar control, blood pressure, erectile function and side effects of cancer including nausea, vomiting and fatigue.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Insomnia is the most frequently noted side effect of Ginseng. Ginseng has a stimulating effect, which is not surprising. Other side effects include increased heart rate, changes in blood pressure and GI symptoms like loss of appetite or diarrhea. Potentially hormonally mediated changes such as breast pain and vaginal bleeding in women have been noted. Rash, itching, mood changes, headache, dizziness, and other problems have also been noted.

ingredients.label_expression:
Withania somnifera (Ashwagandha) root extract
equivalent Withania somnifera dry root
equivalent withanolides
ingredients.max_dose_justification:
Therapeutic range 3-6 gm daily MT TR 300mg - 600 mg of a 10:1 extract UL https://www.researchgate.net/publication/232814045_Exploratory_study_to_evaluate_tolerability_safety_and_activity_of_Ashwagandha_Withania_somnifera_in_healthy_volunteers 1250 mg, 8:1 =10000 mg. Divided so brought down UL slightly.
ML - Based on 10:1 extract and traditional use equivalent to 5g dry root in a single serve. Typically used at 3-10g per day

clinical_details.introduction: Ashwagandha is an herb that has been used for centuries in India. It is known as an adaptogen which relates to its stress modulation effects. It has been used in a wide variety of conditions from insomnia to supporting sexual function and preserving cognitive health.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Ashwagandha may cause miscarriages and hence should be avoided in pregnancy. It may lower blood pressure and blood sugar which can cause dizziness or other problems. Ashwagandha may cause irritation of the GI tract particularly in people with stomach ulcers. It may also alter thyroid function and can possibly stimulate the immune system in ways that are inadvisable in certain autoimmune conditions.

ingredients.label_expression:
Astragalus membranaceus (Astragalus) root extract
equivalent Astragalus membranaceus dry root
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 6-15 gm daily MT: 2-30 gm daily Braun and Cohen TR 150 mg - 900 mg of a 20:1 extract TR between MT & BC UL at BC top range

clinical_details.introduction: Astragalus has been used as a dietary supplement for many conditions, including for diarrhea, fatigue, anorexia, upper respiratory infections, heart disease, hepatitis, fibromyalgia, and as an adjunctive therapy for cancer. It has been widely studied in a number of conditions [1-8]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects are generally mild and related to the GI tract with diarrhea being the most commonly observed side effect. The possibility of lowering blood pressure and blood sugar below levels that are desirable is also a potential concern especially for patients on medications that lower blood pressure and blood sugar.

ingredients.label_expression:
Eleutherococcus senticosus (Siberian ginseng) root extract
equivalent Eleutherococcus senticosus dry root
equivalent eleutherosides
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 3-4 gm daily MT TR 1-4 gm day BC TR 70 mg to 270 mg of a 15:1 extract 9BC reference range, UL reflects traditional dosing)

clinical_details.introduction: Eleutherococcus senticosus is a plant that has been used to improve general wellbeing and overall health. As a general tonic and adaptogen, it is used to relieve or mitigate stress and to improve quality of life in older individuals.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects may include lowered blood pressure. Changes in cortisol hormones have been reported. Increased bleeding is possible. Allergic reactions such as hives have also been reported.
clinical_details.references:
1.Schaffler K, Wolf OT, et al. No benefit adding eleutherococcus senticosus to stress management training in stress-related fatigue/weakness, impaired work or concentration, a randomized controlled study. Pharmacopsychiatry. 2013 Jul;46(5):181-190.
2.Hartz AJ, Bentler S, et al. Randomized controlled trial of Siberian ginseng for chronic fatigue. Psychol Med. 2004 Jan;34(1):51-61.

ingredients.label_expression:
Camellia sinensis (Green tea) leaf extract
equivalent Camellia sinensis dry leaf
equivalent EGCG
equivalent caffeine
ingredients.tga_restrictions:
Caffeine is a mandatory component of Camellia sinensis. 
When the medicine is packaged for supply as a divided preparation and is for internal use or oral application, the medicine must not contain a concentration of total caffeine greater than 33%. 
When for internal use or oral application, the maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine must provide no more than 400 mg of total caffeine. 
When the medicine is packaged for supply as an undivided preparation and is for internal use or oral application, the medicine must not contain a 
concentration of total caffeine greater than 1%. 
When the medicine is for internal use or oral application, a maximum recommended dose of the medicine must not provide more than 100 mg of 
total caffeine within a 3 hour period.
ingredients.tga_warnings:
When the maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine provides greater than 10 mg of total caffeine and the medicine is for internal use or oral application, the following warning statements are required on the label: - (ADULT) 'Adults only' (or words to that effect). - (CAFF) 'Contains [state quantity per dosage unit or per mL or per gram of product] total caffeine [per dosage unit or per mL or per gram]. A cup of instant coffee contains approximately 80mg of caffeine.' - (CAFFPREG) ‘Caffeine intake more than 200 mg per day is not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.’ 
When the maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine provides greater than 80 mg of total caffeine and the medicines is for internal use or oral application, the following warning statements are required on the label: - (CAFFLMT) 'Limit the use of caffeine-containing products (including tea and coffee) when taking this product.' - (CAFFCYP) ‘Caffeine interacts with enzyme CYP1A2 in the liver. Consult your health professional before taking with other medicines’ (or words to that effect). 
When used in oral medicines, the following warning statements are required on
 the medicine label: - 'In rare cases, Camellia sinensis may harm the liver. Stop use and see a 
doctor if you have yellowing skin/eyes, or unusual: fatigue, nausea, appetite loss, abdominal pain, dark urine, or itching.'; and - (FOOD) ‘To be taken with food.’ unless when: 
(a) the preparation of Camellia sinensis is derived from an aqueous extract and
 contains 300 mg or less epigallocatechin-3-gallate per maximum 
recommended daily dose; or 
(b) Camellia sinensis is used in combination with other permitted ingredients as a flavour proprietary excipient formulation. 
The total concentration of flavour proprietary excipient formulations containing 
Camellia sinensis must not be more than 5% of the total medicine.
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 1.5 - 3 gm daily MT toxicity of EGCG at >800 mg daily.  TR should read 100-200 mg of a 15:1 extract UL 1500 mg of extract

clinical_details.introduction: EGCG is the most active ingredient identified in Green Tea. It is a catechin class compound and has strong antiinflammatory effects in both the brain and the body. It has polyphenol antioxidant compounds that have antioxidant, anticarcinogenic, and antimicrobial properties [1,2]. Green tea is popular from a health standpoint for preventing cancer and heart disease, but solid scientific evidence is not yet available [3-11].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Liver toxicity and kidney toxicity have been reported apparently only with doses over 800 mg. per day. Dizziness and low blood sugar as well as anemia are also possible side effects.

ingredients.label_expression:
Allium sativum (Garlic) bulb extract
equivalent Allium sativum dry bulb
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range  TR 300 - 1500 mg/day BC TR 10mg - 80mg of 20:1 extract                                             UL https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17325296/

clinical_details.introduction: Garlic or Allium sativum is a plant grown for its strongly flavored bulbs. It is known to have many health benefits.  Garlic is most often consumed as a supplement as an extract standardized to a specific concentration of one of the main active ingredients allicin. Its main beneficial effects relate to general overall cardiovascular disease prevention, control of blood pressure and lipid lowering. It also has strong digestive properties.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Garlic may cause upset stomach, heartburn, vomiting and diarrhea. It may also cause breath and body odor. Additionally, garlic may increase the risk of bleeding.

ingredients.label_expression:
Vitis vinifera (Grape) seed extract
equivalent Vitis vinifera dry seed
equivalent proanthocyanidins
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 150- 400 mg daily BC (extract, need to check the ratio) cites endotelon need to confirm the ratio TR 150 - 400 mg of a 120:1 Matt confirmed

clinical_details.introduction:
The active compounds in Grape seed extract include the strong antioxidant oligomeric proanthocyanidin (OPC), which has been widely studied.
Grape seed extract is used as a dietary supplement for different clinical conditions including venous insufficiency and to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body. Grape seed extract also appears to reduce inflammation. It decreases oxidative stress and may be beneficial for individuals with fatty liver. Grape seed extract may also be of value in venous disorders including venous insufficiency [1,3,4,8].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Some potential side effects may include headache, scalp itchiness, dizziness and nausea. There may also be a potential for liver damage.
Side effects of glutathione include bloating and abdominal cramps. Allergic reactions and respiratory distress due to bronchial constriction are also reported.

Tagetes erecta (Aztec marigold) flower extract
equivalent Tagetes erecta dry flower
equivalent lutein
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 80-270 mg mg/day of a 7:1 extract (gives 6-20 mg of lutein) reference BC

clinical_details.introduction: Lutein is a carotenoid antioxidant found in green and yellow vegetables. It is a strong antioxidant that protects against cellular damage.  There may be an important role that lutein and related compounds play in protecting the retina against damage that can lead to macular degeneration. Weaker evidence suggests a possible protective role against cataract formation or progression.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Lutein appears to have minimal to no side effects. As with any substance rare allergic reactions are possible. In excessive doses when combined with zeaxanthin may turn skin yellow. Long term supplement use in high-risk group such as smokers with beta-carotene and lutein appear to increase lung cancer risk. Whether this applies to low-risk populations  or whether population effects are explained by subsets of high risk individuals is less clear

ingredients.label_expression: Zeaxanthin
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 2-5 mg/day (BC, says 2-5 well tolerated)

clinical_details.introduction:
Zeaxanthin is plant derived compound that has a special affinity for the eye, especially the 
macula. It helps to protect the eye from the harmful effects of light, especially blue light. It is also, a strong antioxidant and protect the critical structures of the eye including the macula and the lens from oxidative damage. It is used to protect the eye from age-related macular degeneration, cataracts, an inflammatory eye condition called uveitis and from diabetic retinopathy [1].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The only commonly reported side effect is change in skin coloring with long term use of high doses.

Silybum marianum (Milk thistle) fruit extract
equivalent Silybum marianum dry fruit
equivalent silymarin
ingredients.max_dose_justification:
Therapeutic range 6-12 gm daily MT  - 200 mg of extract 100 - 170 mg of a 70:1 extract
YSCP confirmed 450mg daily is fine. Clinical trials go up to 1500mg in clinical studies. https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12906-025-04886-y

clinical_details.introduction:
Milk thistle is a bushy plant that has a long history of use for its salutary effects on the liver.
It has been found to have both protective and restorative effects on the liver in regard to both infections and damage from toxins from alcohol to a number of different medications that can damage the liver. It has been approved in Germany in extract form as a treatment for liver disease since the 1960s. Silymarin is an extract of milk thistle and itself contains at least 7 different chemicals. The active ingredients in silymarin appear to act as antioxidants and may contribute to the regeneration of injured liver tissue [1-3].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Most of the reported side effects are gastrointestinal in nature – nausea, bloating, vomiting, gas, diarrhea, and abdominal discomfort. Headache is also reported as well as allergic reactions especially for those individuals allergic to ragweed.

clinical_details.introduction: Pycnogenol is an extract of French Maritime Pine Bark. It has a group of substances called oligomeric proanthocyanidin complexes (OPCs). They are used most often for preventing and treating venous disease.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Reported side effects may include dizziness, headache, gastrointestinal complaints and mouth ulcers.

clinical_details.introduction: Pomegranate is a fruit that contains extraordinarily strong antioxidant and antiinflammatory compounds including punicalagins and punicic acid [1]. These have proven efficacy in supporting the cardiovascular system, in slowing the growth of certain neoplasms such as prostate cancer and in lowering generalized inflammation in the body.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Pomegranate is generally extremely well tolerated with few side effects. Rare allergic reactions with itching, swelling, nasal discharge and respiratory difficulties have been reported.Pomegranate has been consumed as food for thousands of years.

ingredients.label_expression: Resveratrol
ingredients.tga_restrictions: The maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine must not contain more than 150 milligrams of resveratrol.
ingredients.tga_warnings: The following warning statements are required on the medicine label: - (RESVER) 'Resveratrol may affect the way some medicines work, including Warfarin. Consult your health professional before taking with other medicines (or words to that effect).';- (PREGNT) ‘Not recommended for use by pregnant and lactating women’ (or words to that effect)’; and- (CHILD2) ‘Not suitable for children’.
ingredients.max_dose_justification:
TR 150-500 mg daily common dosage, higher doses at 2 gm daily
 TR 150 -1000 mg daily, UL 5gm https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/25/2/747?utm
YSCP confirmed 600mg Therapeutic Clinical use (most effective) (Chen S et al. 2015). 1000mg Aggressive Metabolic use. (Brown VA et al. 2010 (Cancer Research)1000mg to 1500mg Upper tolerable long-term.

clinical_details.introduction: Rhodiola is a plant known to grow in the mountainous areas of Asia and Europe. The root is used as a medicinal agent in traditional cultures. It is most commonly used as a supplement as an adaptogen for stress and also for fatigue and anxiety. It may also have a role as an agent to support mood and help depression.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects from Rhodiola are typically uncommon and tend to be mild. The most frequent side effects are agitation, insomnia, and anxiety. Headache, allergic reactions and increased blood pressure have also been reported as has chest pain.

clinical_details.introduction: Boswellia is an antiinflammatory agent derived from a tree. It has been used for a number of painful and inflammatory conditions. The most consistent evidence for its use is in osteoarthritis.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The most frequently reported side effects include nausea, heart burn, stomach pain, diarrhea, headaches, and rashes.

clinical_details.introduction: Betaine is a key metabolic intermediate for methylation and in homocysteine metabolism. It has been FDA approved as a supplement to decrease elevated homocysteine. It may also have antiinflammatory effects but whether these are modulated through its effect on homocysteine is unclear [1].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The most common side effects are nausea, stomach upset, diarrhea and body odor. It may also increase total and LDL cholesterol.

ingredients.label_expression:
Glycyrrhiza glabra (Liquorice) root extract
equivalent Glycyrrhiza glabra dry root
equivalent glycyrrhizin
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 1.5-6 gm daily ABC (Murray and Pizorno) 3.8 gm for ulcers BC in 5 divided doses I think we can stay at 300 mg because it is 1 daily dose.  TR is 150mg - 300 mg of a 6:1 extract daily, UL is higher because of safety of DGL

clinical_details.introduction: Licorice has a compound in it called glycyrrhizin which inhibits the breakdown of cortisol and hence increases cortisol in the body. This has been shown to have some beneficial effects on energy and anti - inflammatory effects. It can however, cause a hyperaldosteronism type syndrome including hypertension and hyperkalaemia. Deglycyrrhizinated Licorice [DGL] came about when it was discovered that some of the salutary effects of Licorice upon the upper GI tract were not dependent upon the corticosteroid effect but instead upon other compounds in the whole herb. Subsequently, DGL has been marketed as a safer version of licorice root activity without the corticosteroid effects. Thus, it avoids these side effects at the cost of losing systemic and topical anti - inflammatory effects. The non - deglycyrrhizinated form of licorice may also have hormonal estrogenic effects and potentially lower testosterone [1,2,3,4,5,10,11,12,13,14,15].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Use of whole licorice, Glycyrrhiza glabra, has been associated with prolonged use with the development of hyperaldosteronism type effects. These include the induction of hypertension and hypokalemia. Whole licorice also seems to lower testosterone and to possess estrogen like effects. DGL does not have the aldosterone like effects. It is unclear whether it may have a lesser degree or any estrogen or testosterone related effects, but the absence of mineralocorticoid effects of whole licorice is an optimistic note suggesting that it may not have these other unwanted effects.

Side effects of DGL itself are limited in the literature to mention of possible allergic reactions and drug interactions.

clinical_details.introduction: Angelica sinensis, popularly called dong quai, is a plant with a cluster of white flowers. The dried root has been used as a traditional medicine in Asia for centuries. Currently the two most common uses are for the symptoms of menopause and for decreasing the pain of menstrual cramps.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Dong quai may cause increased sun sensitivity and tendency to get sunburn. It may increase bleeding tendencies. It should be avoided in pregnant and nursing women because of concerns over risks to the baby. Lowered blood pressure, lowered blood sugar, sweating and headache have also been reported.

clinical_details.introduction: Berberine is often sourced from barberry, Oregon grape and goldenseal which themselves are used as dietary supplements. The primary focus of research on berberine has been in metabolic disorders including regulation of blood sugar and associated aspects of metabolic syndrome including improving lipid profiles. It also has potent antioxidant and antiinflammatory effects.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects are primarily GI in nature and include diarrhea, constipation, and stomach pain. It has also been reported to cause uterine contractions and should be avoided during pregnancy.

ingredients.tga_restrictions:
The route of administration for medicines that contain Lepidium meyenii must be limited to oral.                                              The ingredient must consist of the dried tuber of Lepidium meyenii only.
The maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine must not provide more than 3.5 g of Lepidium meyenii dried tuber (or its extract equivalent).
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 2- 3.5 gm daily. Toxicity studies show safety at 7.5 g/kg (rats) and 15g/kg on mice, suggesting it is safe in high doses (Meissner, 2006) BC TR is 700mg to 3,500 mg of a 18:1 extract daily

clinical_details.introduction: Maca is a root vegetable that originated in Peru and has been used as a traditional medicine. It has been studied to a limited degree with potentially good results for a number of conditions from prostate enlargement to lowering blood pressure but the only indication that has achieved widespread use is for the enhancement of libido. It also appears to increase fertility.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Interaction with thyroid problems is a potential concern as maca is a potential goiter - inducing agent.
Using maca in patients with estrogen - dependent neoplasia is also a possible concern.

clinical_details.introduction:
Historically, turmeric was used in Ayurveda and other traditional Indian medical systems, as well as Eastern Asian medical systems such as traditional Chinese medicine. In India, it was traditionally used for disorders of the skin, upper respiratory tract, joints, and digestive system.
Today, turmeric is promoted as a dietary supplement for a variety of conditions, including arthritis, digestive disorders, respiratory infections, allergies, liver disease, depression, and many others. 
Turmeric is a common spice and a major ingredient in curry powder. Curcumin is a major component of turmeric, and the activities of turmeric are commonly attributed to curcuminoids (curcumin and closely related substances). Curcumin gives turmeric its yellow color.
Turmeric dietary supplements are made from the dried rhizome and typically contain a mixture of curcuminoids. Turmeric is also made into a paste for skin conditions.
Curcumin is marketed in various forms that include the active curcuminoids. Bioavailability is a problem with turmeric and also with curcumin. Various strategies have been used to enhance bioavailability [1-6].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Curcumin may cause upset stomach, acid reflux and diarrhea as well as allergic reactions. Iron deficiency has been noted along with increased gallbladder problems and lowering of blood sugar which can be problematic in certain individuals at risk or taking other medicines or agents which lower blood sugar.

clinical_details.introduction: The most common useful effects of Fenugreek relate to improvements in glucose metabolism. Either in addition to or because of improved glucose metabolism Fenugreek also has beneficial effects on lipid parameters such as HDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels. Fenugreek was also found to be liver protective in certain experimental models. [1,2,3]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: GI side effects such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea and abdominal discomfort are reported. It can also cause allergic reactions that may include facial swelling, wheezing and more severe allergic reactions. A “maple syrup” odor in the urine and headaches have also been observed. Since fenugreek is reported to lower blood sugar those who have problems with hypoglycemia or who take pharmacologic agents to lower their blood sugar may be at risk of excessive lowering of blood sugar values.

ingredients.label_expression:
Quercetin dihydrate
equivalent quercetin
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 150mg-2000 gm daily. Well tolerated and appears to be associated with little toxicity orally or IV (Lamson and Brignall 2000) BC UL 2gm

clinical_details.introduction: Quercetin is a compound that gives plants and foods yellowish coloration. It is found in apples, onions, and in some teas. It has been used for cardiovascular health and has shown beneficial effects on both blood pressure and metabolic syndrome. It may be of value in improving recovery and symptoms with upper respiratory tract infections.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects are listed as headaches and numbness and tingling of the extremities. Very high dose or intravenous use may cause kidney damage.

ingredients.label_expression:
Cinnamomum cassia (Cinnamon) bark extract
equivalent Cinnamomum cassia dry bark
equivalent polyphenols
ingredients.tga_restrictions:
Cassia oil is a mandatory component of Cinnamomum cassia if the plant 
preparation is an essential oil, distillate, fixed oil or infused oil.
The concentration of Cassia oil in the medicine must be no more than 2%.
When used as an active ingredient, the concentration of coumarin in the medicine must be no more than 0.001%.
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 1 - 6 gm daily (BC) 1.5-4 gm up to 4 times daily (BC) Generally well tolerated (BC) TR 250 mg - 1000 mg of a 4:1 extract daily No UL

clinical_details.introduction:
There are many types of cinnamon. Ceylon cinnamon (Cinnamomum verum), grown primarily in Sri Lanka, is known as “true” cinnamon. Cassia cinnamon (Cinnamomum aromaticum), grown in southeastern Asia, is the most common type sold in North America.
Used as a spice for thousands of years, cinnamon comes from the bark of various species of cinnamon trees. The leaves, flowers, fruits, and roots of cinnamon trees have also been used in cooking and for medicinal purposes. There are differences in the chemical composition of cinnamon products produced from different species or parts of cinnamon trees.
                     Cinnamon has a long history of use in traditional medicine in various parts of the world,   
                     including China, India, and Persia (Iran).
Today, cinnamon is promoted as a dietary supplement for diabetes or for irritable bowel syndrome or other gastrointestinal problems, as well as other conditions. Cassia cinnamon is promoted for topical use (application to the skin) as an insect repellent.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Cinnamon can possibly lower blood sugar below the desired range in some individuals, especially in those prone to low blood sugars or who are taking pharmaceutical agents that lower blood sugar. It can also cause topical rashes or allergic reactions.

Cassia cinnamon can potentially be toxic to the liver due to an ingredient called coumarin that is present in some cinnamon products. Water soluble extracts are generally free of this toxin.

ingredients.active_ingredient: Lemon balm (Melissa officinalis) extract (standardized to 3% rosmaric acid)
ingredients.label_expression:
Melissa officinalis (Lemon balm) herb extract
equivalent Melissa officinalis dry herb
equivalent rosmarinic acid
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 6-12 gm daily MT TR 4.5- 13.5 gm/day BC TR is 400 mg - 3000 mg of a 4:1 daily extract  ROUNDED DOWN to even No. of granules

clinical_details.introduction: Lemon balm is a plant derived product. The leaves of the plant give off a distinctive lemon odor when bruised or crushed. The plant has been found to have sedative properties and also has demonstrated activity in shortening the duration of and possibly preventing recurrence of HSV1 and HSV2 infections.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Reported side effects include headache, changes in body temperature, wheezing and painful urination. In addition stomach pain, nausea, vomiting and dizziness have also been reported.

Passiflora incarnata (Passionflower) herb extract
equivalent Passiflora incarnata dry herb
equivalent flavonoids
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 2-8 gm daily MT TR 200 - 700 mg daily of a 10:1 extract, UL 800 mg MT

clinical_details.introduction: Passionflower is a plant that has purple flowers. These have been used in traditional medicine for anxiety and insomnia and current use also is for anxiety and insomnia along with mood support. It is taken either as a supplement, a tincture or a tea most commonly.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The most commonly reported side effects are dizziness, confusion and drowsiness. Liver and pancreatic toxicity have also been reported as have nausea and vomiting.

ingredients.label_expression:
Eschscholzia californica (California poppy) herb extract
equivalent Eschscholzia californica dry herb
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 300-1000 mg/day DEE BHP TR 300mg - 600 mg of a 4:1 extract daily, UL 1000 mg

clinical_details.introduction: California poppy is a cousin of the other poppy species around the world but is devoid of narcotic compounds. It does however have pain relieving properties and can be used for stress and mood disorders as well as a sedative. It contains a number of bioactive alkaloids including papaverine, noscapine, and tubocurarine, among others. They have been shown to be related to the antiinfalmmatory and pain-relieving effects. It has significant interactions with GABA receptors and may also modulate adrenergic pathways [References 1,2]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The most common side effect is sedation. Could potentially mask the effects of an underlying disorder if used to relieve pain. Allergic reactions are possible to plant derived products. False positive tests for opiates have been reported with this agent. If taken during the night for nighttime awakening or if used at higher doses the possibility of morning grogginess exists.

ingredients.label_expression:
Styphnolobium japonicum (Japanese pagoda tree) fruit extract
equivalent Styphnolobium japonicum dry fruit
equivalent genistein
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR is 30mg - 90 mg of a 100:1 extract daily,  UL 150 mg of a 100:1 extract based on a genistein level

clinical_details.introduction: Genistein is a compound derived from soy that has estrogenic properties. It is a plant estrogen, or phytoestrogen and as such many of its benefits derive from stimulating estrogen receptors. Phytoestrogens as opposed to endogenously or exogenously derived estrogens. Phytoestrogens interact more with E2 Estrogen receptors than E1 so they have a different safety profile yet maintain cardiovascular health benefits, the ability to positively influence bone density and effectiveness in improving the symptoms of menopausal hot flashes. [References 1]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: As many as 20% of people taking genistein have reported GI side effects such as nausea, abdominal discomfort, and bloating.  There are theoretical risks of giving genistein in individuals with estrogen dependent cancers, however there are also reasons that genistein could be protective against certain cancers.

ingredients.active_ingredient: Holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum) extract (standardized to 2.5% ursolic acid)
ingredients.label_expression:
Ocimum tenuiflorum (Holy basil) leaf extract
equivalent Ocimum tenuiflorum dry leaf
equivalent ursolic acid
ingredients.tga_restrictions:
When the plant part is oil or distillate, eugenol is a mandatory component of Ocimum tenuiflorum. 
When the concentration of eugenol in the preparation is more than 25% and 
the nominal capacity of the container is more than 15 millilitres but less than or 
equal to 25 millilitres, the medicine must have a child resistant closure and 
restricted flow insert fitted on the container. 
When the concentration of eugenol in the preparation is more than 25% and the nominal capacity of the container is no more than 15 millilitres, the medicine must have a restricted flow insert fitted on the container. 
When the preparation is for topical use in the mouth, the preparation may not contain more than 5 mL of eugenol and the concentration of eugenol in the product must not be greater than 25%.
ingredients.tga_warnings: When the concentration of eugenol in the preparation is more than 25%, the nominal capacity of the container must not be more than 25 millilitres and the following warning statements must be included on the medicine label: - (CHILD) 'Keep out of reach of children' (or words to that effect); and - (NTAKEN) 'Not to be taken'.
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 300mg -  500 mg of a 10:1 extract daily, UL 1200 mg

clinical_details.introduction: Holy Basil is an herb whose use goes back to Ayurvedic traditional medicine. Holy Basil is said to be an adaptogen that helps the body respond to stress. It is an antioxidant and also has specific beneficial effects for mood and anxiety issues as well as the potential to lower blood sugar.  It is also an antioxidant. [Reference 1,2,3,4]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Upset stomach and nausea, lowering of blood sugar, increased bleeding and lowered fertility have all been reported.

ingredients.active_ingredient: Saw Palmetto (Serenoa repens) extract (standardized to 30% fatty acids)

Serenoa repens (Saw palmetto) fruit extract
equivalent Serenoa repens dry fruit
equivalent fatty acids
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 1.5 - 3 gm daily MT TR 200 mg - 400 mg of a 8:1 extract daily, UL 640 mg of a 10:1 extract daily

clinical_details.introduction: Saw palmetto has biological properties of being a 5-alpha reductase inhibitor. The enzyme 5-alpha reductase converts testosterone into DHT [dihydrotestosterone]. Since dihydrotestosterone can cause prostate enlargement and baldness blocking this enzyme can decrease both of these problems.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Reported side effects include nausea and vomiting and other GI symptoms such as diarrhea and constipation. Dizziness, headache, and sexual symptoms including impotence have been reported. Very rare cases of liver damage have been reported.

clinical_details.introduction: Chinese Skullcap is an herbal agent that has active antioxidant and antiinflammatory components. The active compounds are flavones including baicalin and baicalein. It has been used for a number of conditions most notably for anxiety and as an antiinflammatory compound. It also may have neuroprotective effects and blood sugar lowering effects.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects may include symptoms such as irregular heartbeat nervous tics,anxiety,drowsiness,and confusion and anxiety.Liver injury has also been reported.

Glycyrrhiza glabra (Liquorice) root extract
equivalent Glycyrrhiza glabra dry root
equivalent glycyrrhizic acid
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 3-15 gm daily MT ABC 150 mg - 300 mg of a 10:1 extract daily, equiv to ga of 78 mg mg (FSA and SCF safety for GA is 100 mg) ul 385 mg

clinical_details.introduction: Licorice root is a biologically active herbal preparation. The primary active component appears to be glycyrrhizin which has corticosteroid - like properties, apparently through inhibiting the breakdown of certain corticosteroids. It has been used for upper GI complaints such as gastroesophageal reflux disease [GERD] and Dyspepsia for millennia. It also has anti - infective properties and may help lessen the effects of URI. Additionally, its anti - inflammatory properties are useful in skin conditions such as eczema. Its antioxidant properties have even demonstrated decreases in LDL cholesterol oxidation [Oxidized - LDL].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Licorice root in excess has corticosteroid and in particular mineralocorticoid-like properties and can lead to blood pressure elevations, low potassium, muscle weakness and heart irregularities.

ingredients.label_expression:
Zingiber officinale (Ginger) root extract
equivalent Zingiber officinale dry root
ingredients.tga_warnings:

equivalent dry weight per dosage unit is equal to or more than 2g, the medicine
 requires the following warning statement on the medicine label: - (GINGER) 'Individuals taking anticoagulants should seek medical advice before taking this medicine.' AND 'Individuals at risk of bleeding problems should seek advice from their healthcare practitioner prior to taking this medicine'.

Therapeutic range .75-3 gm daily MT . TR 1-3 gm/daily BC TR is 50 - 150 mg of a 20:1 extract daily, UL 200 mg of a 20:1 extract 
ML - Reduced from 250 to 50. 20:1 extract is equivalent to 1gm ginger. Changed max dose from 750 to 250 equivalent to 5g dry ginger root for single serve is in line with traditional recommendations.

clinical_details.introduction: Ginger is an agent that has long been used as a cooking spice in many cultures. It has also been shown to have medicinal properties. Its most common use is for stomach upset and nausea. It also has antiinflammatory effects and is used alone or in combination with other agents for improving the symptoms of osteoarthritis. Additionally, it has been shown to have promotility effects on the GI tract which may in part be responsible for its antinausea activity. [Reference 1]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects from ginger may include heartburn, nausea, diarrhea, and increased bleeding risk including heavier menstrual bleeding in women.

clinical_details.introduction: Rosemary is an herb used cooking for flavoring and cooking throughout much of the world. It has been found to have significant medical uses particularly in regard to enhancing memory and improving cognition. It also has strong antioxidant and antiinflammatory activity.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Rosemary does have a significant number of side effects especially at high doses some of which are extremely serious. Stomach and intestinal irritation as well as renal damage, seizures, pulmonary edema and miscarriage have all been reported as have coma. This agent should be avoided by pregnant women.

ingredients.label_expression:
Apium graveolens (Celery) seed extract
equivalent Apium graveolens dry seed
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapeutic range 1.5 - 9 gm daily MT TR is 250 - 1000 mg of 6:1 extract daily,

clinical_details.introduction: Celery seed is a common spice but is also used as an herbal supplement. Its primary use is as a diuretic. This action may also be used as part of detoxification treatments. Celery seed does not come from the common celery we eat as a vegetable but rather from an ancestor plant of the current celery in use as a vegetable known as wild celery. It may also have some degree of antifungal effects. It also may have antiinflammatory and antioxidant effects. [References 1,2,3,4]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects of celery seed extract are not well studied. There is some concern that it may be of risk to individuals with renal inflammation. It also has a number of potential drug interactions.

ingredients.label_expression:
Bacopa monnieri (Bacopa) herb extract
equivalent Bacopa monnieri dry herb
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Therapuetic range 3-6 gm daily MT. TR 5-10 g/day BC TR 150-500 mg of a 20:1 extract daily, UL 600 mg

clinical_details.introduction: Bacopa monneri is a traditional medicinal that has been used in a wide variety of conditions. These include use for depression and anxiety, as an antiinflammatory and antioxidant, to decrease or prevent seizures, and most consistently to improve cognitive function or prevent cognitive decline.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects are predominantly related to the Gastrointestinal tract such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea and upset stomach. Other concerns such as slow heart rates, fatigue, drowsiness, dry mouth, and thyroid issues have been reported. Contamination with heavy metals has also been an issue with certain supplements including Bacopa.

ingredients.tga_restrictions:
Caffeine is a mandatory component of Coffea arabica.
When the medicine is packaged for supply as a divided preparation and is for internal use or oral application, the medicine must not contain a concentration of total caffeine greater than 33%.
When for internal use or oral application, the maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine must provide no more than 400 mg of total caffeine.
When the medicine is packaged for supply as an undivided preparation and is for internal use or oral application, the medicine must not contain a concentration of total caffeine greater than 1%.                                                             When the medicine is for internal use or oral application, a maximum recommended dose of the medicine must not provide more than 100 mg of total caffeine within a 3 hour period.
ingredients.tga_warnings:
When the maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine provides greater than 10 mg of total caffeine and the medicine is for internal use or oral application, the following warning statements are required on the label: - (ADULT) 'Adults only' (or words to that effect). - (CAFF) 'Contains [state quantity per dosage unit or per mL or per gram of product] total caffeine [per dosage unit or per mL or per gram]. A cup of instant coffee contains approximately 80 mg of caffeine.' - (CAFFPREG) ‘Caffeine intake more than 200 mg per day is not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.’
When the maximum recommended daily dose of the medicine provides greater than 80 mg of total caffeine and the medicines is for internal use or oral application, the following warning statements are required on the label: - (CAFFLMT) 'Limit the use of caffeine-containing products (including tea and coffee) when taking this product.' - (CAFFCYP) ‘Caffeine interacts with enzyme CYP1A2 in the liver. Consult your
 health professional before taking with other medicines’ (or words to that effect).

clinical_details.introduction: The same plant that produces coffee beans that make the ever-popular drink also produces berries. These berries have long been discarded but recently have been discovered to have strong antioxidant and other properties that are beneficial to health.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Side effects of coffee fruit extract include overstimulation effects such as nervousness, being restless and insomnia. GI upset such as stomach distress, nausea and vomiting have been reported. Coffee Fruit Extract can also cause rapid heart rates or rapid breathing.

clinical_details.introduction:
Sage is an herb that is used as a seasoning in cooking but has also been discovered to have significant benefits in several areas including improving Alzheimer’s dementia, diabetes, hyperlipidemia as well as menopausal symptoms. 
[reference 1]
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: One of the main concerns about sage is that some extractions contain the compound thujone which can be toxic especially if taken over an extended period. Sage can trigger seizures in susceptible individuals, perhaps due to its thujone constituent. Sage can also lower blood sugar which especially if in combination with Rx agents that lower sugar could cause sugars low enough to be a risk.

clinical_details.introduction: Bergamot is a compound that is extracted from the rind of the fruit of a specific variety of the Seville orange tree which grows in Italy. Bergamot is different from other citrus extracts. Bergamot contains polyphenols, flavonoids and pectins that can have a beneficial effect on health. Specifically flavonoids such as brutieridin and melitidin as well as flavonoid O-glucosides and C-glucosides. It is the distinctive flavoring used in Earl Grey tea, and is also used as an essential oil for aromatherapy to relieve anxiety. Bergamot as an oral supplement has been used extensively and has good evidence for treating elevated blood lipid levels. It may also reduce inflammation, treat joint pain, and lower blood glucose levels. The flavonoids and other phytochemicals in bergamot promote immune response and heart health. The mechanisms that are related to bergamot’s anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects include chelation of metal ions, inhibition of enzymes producing reactive oxygen species, upregulation of endogenous antioxidant proteins, stimulation of autophagy of hepatic lipid droplets, reduction of DNA oxidative damage, inhibition of proinflammatory cytokine production, and reduction of lipid peroxidation. [References 1,2, 3, 4].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Bergamot was well tolerated in trials.  Side effects of bergamot, if any are mild but heartburn was reported in some of the clinical trials.  Bergamot might make the skin more sensitive to sunlight especially if used in conjunction with photosensitizing medications.

clinical_details.introduction: Coleus forskohlii is native to India and has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for centuries for the treatment of various health conditions, like respiratory disorders, heart disease, circulation conditions, abdominal colic, and cancers [4,8]. It is a perennial herb in a large genus of mints and is a rich source of terpenoids, flavonoids, and alkaloids [13]. In the mid-1970s, the plant's cardiovascular activity was investigated and it was discovered that diterpene forskolin directly activates adenylate cyclase (AC) – AC is known to relax many smooth muscles, it increases cellular cAMP (cyclic adenosine monophosphate), which is involved in processes such as glycogen and lipid metabolism [2,8].  Additionally, forskolin improves coronary blood flow and myocardial and left ventricular function and increases the blood flow in the cerebrum and kidneys [2].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Orally, intravenously, ophthalmologically, and by inhalation, coleus seems to be well tolerated [1].
•	Calcium Channel Blockers- Using forskolin with calcium channel blockers such as verapamil (Calan, Covera-HS, Verelan), nifedipine (Procardia), and diltiazem (Cardizem, Dilacor, Tiazac) might cause additive coronary vasodilatory effects [1]. 
•	Nitrates - Using forskolin with nitrates such as nitroglycerin (Nitro-Bid, Nitro-Dur, Nitrostat) and isosorbide (Imdur, Isordil, Sorbitrate) might cause additive coronary vasodilatory effects [1]. 
•	Forskolin should be avoided in patients with polycystic kidney disease [2].
•	C. forskohlii and its major constituents may not be involved in CYP450 induction-based drug interaction [5].

ingredients.label_expression:
Elettaria cardamomum (Cardamom) fruit extract
equivalent Elettaria cardamomum dry fruit
ingredients.max_dose_justification:
Agreed TR 
ML - Traditional use 500mg instead of 1000mg

clinical_details.introduction: Green cardamom is part of the Zingiberaceae family, distributed in tropical and subtropical areas, and has been widely used in traditional Chinese and Indian medicine to prevent and treat numerous pathologies [6]. It is often referred to as the "Queen of Spices,” known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer, anti-emetic, anti-microbial properties and for cardiovascular disease & neurodegenerative disease prevention [6]. The main bioactive compounds of cardamom are terpenes and phenolic compounds, which exhibit powerful antioxidant properties. Their main bioactive compounds are as follows: 1,8-cineole, α-terpinyl acetate, nerolidol, sabinene, g-terpinene, α-pinene, methyl linoleate, α-terpineol, β-pinene, n-hexadecanoic acid, and limonene [1].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Cardamom has Generally Recognized as Safe (GRAS) status in the US. Cardamom powder 3 grams daily in 2-3 divided doses has been used with apparent safety for up to 16 weeks. Orally, cardamom seems to be well tolerated.

ingredients.label_expression:
Crocus sativus (Saffron) stigma extract
equivalent Crocus sativus dry stigma
equivalent safranal
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 30 - 100 mg daily BC TR is 30-60 mg of a 3:1 extract daily

clinical_details.introduction: The genus Crocus is a member of the Iridaceae (subfamily Crocoideae) and consists of about 100 species that occur in the wild. These are mainly found in central-southern Europe (Balkan Peninsula), North Africa, and Western Asia [5]. In some Asian countries, traditional knowledge on saffron healing effects dates from prehistoric periods (12th Century BC) [9,10]. Traditionally, Saffron has been used as a tonic to strengthen the vascular & nervous system and for treatment of depression, insomnia, measles, and dysentery [11]. Chemical determinants of the bitterness, scent, and color of saffron are thought to be derived from terpenoid precursors [6]. Other constituents of saffron include the phenolic acids, such as tannic, gallic, caffeic, cinnamic, chlorogenic, ferulic, and vanillic acids [1]. In particular, picrocrocin, safranal and crocetin (a carotenoid) are powerful phytochemicals that are associated with decreased oxidative stress [7]. Safranal is an antioxidant with radical-scavenger properties, and may be useful as an anticonvulsant and antidepressant [8]. Among the saffron constituents, crocetin has the strongest antioxidant activity (DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate)  [7].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Generally well-tolerated. Common side effects from Saffron taken Orally: Gastrointestinal complaints, nausea, sedation, vomiting. In doses that exceed 5 grams symptoms of saffron poisoning include vertigo, numbness, vomiting, bloody diarrhea, hematuria, uremic collapse, bleeding, and thrombocytopenic purpura [1].

ingredients.label_expression:
Cordyceps sinensis (Cordyceps) mycellium extract
equivalent Cordyceps sinensis dry mycellium
equivalent adenosine
ingredients.tga_restrictions: Must not contain material of animal origin such as insect larvae.
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 1-3 gm daily Martin Powell TR is 350-1,000 mg of a 3:1 extract

clinical_details.introduction: Cordyceps Sinensis (CS) is an entomopathogenic fungus found in Asia mountain region [9]. Although it has been traditionally used for hundreds of years, in 1964, Cordyceps sinensis was officially recorded as an herbal drug in Chinese pharmacopoeia. C. sinensis, known as Dongchongxiacao (winter-worm summer-grass) in Chinese, and remains one of the most famous traditional Chinese medicines and medicinal mushrooms [7]. Cordyceps is a rich source of over 200 bioactive compounds, including nucleotides, nucleosides, polysaccharides, proteins, sterols, vitamins (Vit E, K, B1, B2, and B12), and trace elements [13]. Studies show that Cordyceps and its active principles possess a wide range of pharmacological actions, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anti-tumor, antihyperglycemic, anti-apoptotic, immunomodulatory, nephroprotective, and hepatoprotective [2].  It has been used to treat conditions such as night sweating, hyposexuality, hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia, asthenia after severe illness, respiratory disease, renal dysfunction, renal failure, arrhythmias and other heart disease and liver disease [3].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Most common side effects when taken orally: Abdominal discomfort, constipation, diarrhea. When used orally and appropriately. Cordyceps has been used with apparent safety in doses of 3-6 grams daily for up to 1 year [1].

ingredients.label_expression:
Glucosamine sulfate sodium chloride
equivalent glucosamine sulfate
ingredients.max_dose_justification: TR 1000- 1500 mg/day BC upper range?

clinical_details.introduction:
Glucosamine is a compound made in the body from various sugar molecules. It is important to joint health and integrity. Most glucosamine supplements are derived from the shells of shellfish or it can be made in the lab.

In osteoarthritis, the surface layer of cartilage between the bones of a joint wears down. This allows the bones to rub together, which can cause pain and swelling and make it difficult to move the joint. The knees, hips, spine, and hands are the parts of the body most likely to be affected by osteoarthritis.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Glucosamine side effects include nausea, abdominal discomfort, constipation, headache and skin rashes or other allergic reactions. Glucosamine may raise blood sugars. This is a particular concern in patients with borderline or elevated blood sugar levels.

ingredients.label_expression:
Bovine sodium chondroitin sulfate
equivalent chondroitin sulfate
ingredients.max_dose_justification: 800-1200 mg/day. Considered extremely safe with v low incidence of adverse reactions BC TR is 800-1200 mg daily

clinical_details.introduction:
Cartilage is the connective tissue that cushions the ends of bones within the joints. 
Chondroitin is a structural component of cartilage, the tissue that cushions the joints. It is produced naturally in the body. It is also available as a dietary supplement. Researchers have studied the effects of chondroitin individually and in combination with glucosamine, on osteoarthritis, a common type of arthritis that destroys the cartilage in the joints.

In osteoarthritis, the surface layer of cartilage between the bones of a joint wears down. This allows the bones to rub together, which can cause pain and swelling and make it difficult to move the joint. The knees, hips, spine, and hands are the parts of the body most likely to be affected by osteoarthritis.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Chondroitin may cause some mild stomach pain and nausea.Qther side effects that have been reported are diarrhea or constipation,headache,leg swelling,hair loss, skin rash, and irregular heart rhythms. There is a possibility it can interact with blood thinning medications and raise blood sugar in predisposed individuals.

ingredients.label_expression: Ascorbic acid (vitamin C)
ingredients.max_dose_justification: It is not possible to establish a UL for vitamin C, but 1,000 mg/day is a prudent limit. GIT issues most commonly reported issue of high dose C over a short time. Caution in  haemochromotosishttps://www.eatforhealth.gov.au/sites/default/files/2022-04/n35-vitaminc_0.pdf TR is 100-2000 mg daily

clinical_details.introduction:
Vitamin C, also known as L-ascorbic acid, is a water-soluble vitamin that is naturally present in some foods, added to others, and available as a dietary supplement. Humans, unlike most animals, are unable to synthesize vitamin C endogenously, so it is an essential dietary component.
Vitamin C is required for the biosynthesis of collagen, L-carnitine, and certain neurotransmitters; vitamin C is also involved in protein metabolism Collagen is an essential component of connective tissue, which plays a vital role in wound healing. Vitamin C is also an important physiological antioxidant and has been shown to regenerate other antioxidants within the body, including alpha-tocopherol (vitamin E). Ongoing research is examining whether vitamin C, by limiting the damaging effects of free radicals through its antioxidant activity, might help to support cardiovascular health, immune function and improves the absorption of nonheme iron, the form of iron present in plant-based foods. Insufficient vitamin C intake causes scurvy, which is characterized by fatigue or lassitude, widespread connective tissue weakness, and capillary fragility
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Possible side effects include GI upset and diarrhea and potential increased risk of renal stone.

ingredients.label_expression:
Thiamine (vitamin B1)
equivalent thiamine Hydrochloride
ingredients.max_dose_justification:
Agreed TR BC TR 10 - 100 mg daily
YSCP confirmed 600-1500mg pr day (Costantini et al., BMJ Open, 2013 — high-dose thiamine improved fatigue in IBD and MS patients (600–1,500 mg/day)

clinical_details.introduction:
Thiamin (or thiamine) is one of the water-soluble B vitamins. It is also known as vitamin B1. Thiamin is naturally present in some foods, added to some food products, and available as a dietary supplement. This vitamin plays a critical role in energy metabolism and, therefore, in the growth, development, and function of cells [1].
Ingested thiamin from food and dietary supplements is absorbed by the small intestine through active transport at nutritional doses and by passive diffusion at pharmacologic doses [1]. Most dietary thiamin is in phosphorylated forms, and intestinal phosphatases hydrolyze them to free thiamin before the vitamin is absorbed [1]. The remaining dietary thiamin is in free (absorbable) form [1,2]. Humans store thiamin primarily in the liver, but in very small amounts [3]. The vitamin has a short half-life, so people require a continuous supply of it from the diet.
About 80% of the approximately 25–30 mg of thiamin in the adult human body is in the form of thiamin diphosphate (TDP; also known as thiamin pyrophosphate), the main metabolically active form of thiamin. Bacteria in the large intestine also synthesize free thiamin and TDP, but their contribution, if any, to thiamin nutrition is currently unknown [4]. TDP serves as an essential cofactor for five enzymes involved in glucose, amino acid, and lipid metabolism [1,3].
Levels of thiamin in the blood are not reliable indicators of thiamin status. Thiamin status is often measured indirectly by assaying the activity of the transketolase enzyme, which depends on TDP, in erythrocyte hemolysates in the presence and absence of added TDP [3]. The result, known as the “TDP effect,” reflects the extent of unsaturation of transketolase with TDP. The result is typically 0%–15% in healthy people, 15%–25% in those with marginal deficiency, and higher than 25% in people with deficiency. Another commonly used measure of thiamin status is urinary thiamin excretion, which provides data on dietary intakes but not tissue stores [5]. For adults, excretion of less than 100 mcg/day thiamin in urine suggests insufficient thiamin intake, and less than 40 mcg/day indicates an extremely low intake [6].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
There do not appear to be side effects from high doses. 
The body excretes excess amounts of thiamin in the urine [2]. Because of the lack of reports of adverse effects from high thiamin intakes (50 mg/day or more) from food or supplements, the FNB did not establish ULs for thiamin [7]. They hypothesize that the apparent lack of toxicity may be explained by the rapid decline in absorption of thiamin at intakes above 5 mg. However, the FNB noted that in spite of the lack of reported adverse events, excessive intakes of thiamin could have adverse effects.

ingredients.label_expression: Nicotinamide (vitamin B3)
ingredients.max_dose_justification: Large doses of nicotinamide (up to 3,000 mg/day for periods of up to 3 years) appear to be well tolerated, as reported in trials on the possible benefi ts of nicotinamide in patients with, or at risk of developing, diabetes. The NOAEL from these studies is approximately 1,800 mg/day. This value represents the lowest reported dose in a number of high quality trials of (Lampeter et al 1998, Pozilli https://www.eatforhealth.gov.au/nutrient-reference-values/nutrients/niacinb TR 10-1000 mg daily, UL 1800 mg cited daily

clinical_details.introduction:
Niacin (also known as vitamin B3) is one of the water-soluble B vitamins. Niacin is the generic name for nicotinic acid (pyridine-3-carboxylic acid), nicotinamide (niacinamide or pyridine-3-carboxamide), and related derivatives, such as nicotinamide riboside [1-3]. Niacin is naturally present in many foods, added to some food products, and available as a dietary supplement.
All tissues in the body convert absorbed niacin into its main metabolically active form, the coenzyme nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD). More than 400 enzymes require NAD to catalyze reactions in the body, which is more than for any other vitamin-derived coenzyme [1]. NAD is also converted into another active form, the coenzyme nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP), in all tissues except skeletal muscle [4].
Both Niacin and Niacinamide can prevent and treat niacin deficiency. Only Niacin has protective cardiovascular and lipid lowering effects. [5, 6]
NAD and NADP are required in most metabolic redox processes in cells where substrates are oxidized or reduced. NAD is primarily involved in catabolic reactions that transfer the potential energy in carbohydrates, fats, and proteins to adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the cell’s primary energy currency [4]. NAD is also required for enzymes involved in critical cellular functions, such as the maintenance of genome integrity, control of gene expression, and cellular communication [3,4]. NADP, in contrast, enables anabolic reactions, such as the synthesis of cholesterol and fatty acids, and plays a critical role in maintaining cellular antioxidant function.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: Niacin but not niacinamide causes intense flushing reactions that may limit the ability of many patients to use it. Niacin also has the potential for hepatotoxicity which has also been reported with high doses of Niacinamide [15]

ingredients.label_expression:
Calcium pantothenate
equivalent pantothenic acid (vitamin B5)
ingredients.max_dose_justification: A UL cannot be determined at this stage. There are no reports of adverse effects of oral pantothenic acid in either humans or animals on which to base a quantitative estimate. Thus a UL cannot be determined at this stage, but current intakes are unlikely to be associated with adverse health effects. BC cites 20-500 mg agreed TR, TR is 5mg to 500 mg daily

clinical_details.introduction:
Pantothenic acid (also known as vitamin B5) is an essential nutrient that is naturally present in some foods, added to others, and available as a dietary supplement. The main function of this water-soluble B vitamin is in the synthesis of coenzyme A (CoA) and acyl carrier protein [1,2]. CoA is essential for fatty acid synthesis and degradation, transfer of acetyl and acyl groups, and a multitude of other anabolic and catabolic processes [3,4]. Acyl carrier protein’s main role is in fatty acid synthesis [2].
Red blood cells carry pantothenic acid throughout the body [4]. Most pantothenic acid in tissues is in the form of CoA, but smaller amounts are present as acyl carrier protein or free pantothenic acid [1,4].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The FNB was unable to establish ULs for pantothenic acid because there are no reports of pantothenic acid toxicity in humans at high intakes. Some individuals taking large doses of pantothenic acid supplements (e.g., 10 g/day) develop mild diarrhea and gastrointestinal distress, but the mechanism for this effect is not known [1,8].

clinical_details.introduction:
Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin that is naturally present in very few foods, added to others, and available as a dietary supplement. It is also produced endogenously when ultraviolet rays from sunlight strike the skin and trigger vitamin D synthesis. Vitamin D obtained from sun exposure, food, and supplements is biologically inert and must undergo two hydroxylations in the body for activation. The first occurs in the liver and converts vitamin D to 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D], also known as calcidiol. The second occurs primarily in the kidney and forms the physiologically active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)2D], also known as calcitriol [1].
Vitamin D promotes calcium absorption in the gut and maintains adequate serum calcium and phosphate concentrations to enable normal mineralization of bone and to prevent hypocalcemic tetany. It is also needed for bone growth and bone remodeling by osteoblasts and osteoclasts [1,2]. Without sufficient vitamin D, bones can become thin, brittle, or misshapen. Vitamin D sufficiency prevents rickets in children and osteomalacia in adults [1]. Together with calcium, vitamin D also helps protect older adults from osteoporosis.
Vitamin D has other roles in the body, including modulation of cell growth, neuromuscular and immune function, and reduction of inflammation [1,3,4]. Many genes encoding proteins that regulate cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis are modulated in part by vitamin D [1]. Many cells have vitamin D receptors, and some convert 25(OH)D to 1,25(OH)2D.
Serum concentration of 25(OH)D is the best indicator of vitamin D status. It reflects vitamin D produced cutaneously and that obtained from food and supplements [1] and has a fairly long circulating half-life of 15 days [5]. 25(OH)D functions as a biomarker of exposure, but it is not clear to what extent 25(OH)D levels also serve as a biomarker of effect (i.e., relating to health status or outcomes) [1]. Serum 25(OH)D levels do not indicate the amount of vitamin D stored in body tissues
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Vitamin D toxicity can cause non-specific symptoms such as anorexia, weight loss, polyuria, and heart arrhythmias. More seriously, it can also raise blood levels of calcium which leads to vascular and tissue calcification, with subsequent damage to the heart, blood vessels, and kidneys [1]. The use of supplements of both calcium (1,000 mg/day) and vitamin D (400 IU) by postmenopausal women was associated with a 17% increase in the risk of kidney stones over 7 years in the Women’s Health Initiative [30]. A serum 25(OH)D concentration consistently >500 nmol/L (>200 ng/mL) is considered to be potentially toxic [5].

Excessive sun exposure does not result in vitamin D toxicity because the sustained heat on the skin is thought to photodegrade previtamin D3 and vitamin D3 as it is formed [6]. In addition, thermal activation of previtamin D3 in the skin gives rise to various non-vitamin D forms that limit formation of vitamin D3 itself. Some vitamin D3 is also converted to nonactive forms [1]. Intakes of vitamin D from food that are high enough to cause toxicity are very unlikely. Toxicity is much more likely to occur from high intakes of dietary supplements containing vitamin D.

Long-term intakes above the UL increase the risk of adverse health effects [1] (Table 4). Most reports suggest a toxicity threshold for vitamin D of 10,000 to 40,000 IU/day and serum 25(OH)D levels of 500–600 nmol/L (200–240 ng/mL). While symptoms of toxicity are unlikely at daily intakes below 10,000 IU/day, the FNB pointed to emerging science from national survey data, observational studies, and clinical trials suggesting that even lower vitamin D intakes and serum 25(OH)D levels might have adverse health effects over time. The FNB concluded that serum 25(OH)D levels above approximately 125–150 nmol/L (50–60 ng/mL) should be avoided, as even lower serum levels (approximately 75–120 nmol/L or 30–48 ng/mL) are associated with increases in all-cause mortality, greater risk of cancer at some sites like the pancreas, greater risk of cardiovascular events, and more falls and fractures among the elderly. The FNB committee cited research which found that vitamin D intakes of 5,000 IU/day achieved serum 25(OH)D concentrations between 100–150 nmol/L (40–60 ng/mL), but no greater. Applying an uncertainty factor of 20% to this intake value gave a UL of 4,000 IU which the FNB applied to children aged 9 and older and adults, with corresponding lower amounts for younger children.

clinical_details.introduction:
Vitamin A is the name of a group of fat-soluble retinoids, including retinol, retinal, and retinyl esters [1-3]. Vitamin A is involved in immune function, vision, reproduction, and cellular communication [1,4,5]. Vitamin A is critical for vision as an essential component of rhodopsin, a protein that absorbs light in the retinal receptors, and because it supports the normal differentiation and functioning of the conjunctival membranes and cornea [2-4]. Vitamin A also supports cell growth and differentiation, playing a critical role in the normal formation and maintenance of the heart, lungs, kidneys, and other organs [2].
Two forms of vitamin A are available in the human diet: preformed vitamin A (retinol and its esterified form, retinyl ester) and provitamin A carotenoids [1-5]. Preformed vitamin A is found in foods from animal sources, including dairy products, fish, and meat (especially liver). By far the most important provitamin A carotenoid is beta-carotene; other provitamin A carotenoids are alpha-carotene and beta-cryptoxanthin. The body converts these plant pigments into vitamin A. Both provitamin A and preformed vitamin A must be metabolized intracellularly to retinal and retinoic acid, the active forms of vitamin A, to support the vitamin’s important biological functions [2,3]. Other carotenoids found in food, such as lycopene, lutein, and zeaxanthin, are not converted into vitamin A.
The various forms of vitamin A are solubilized into micelles in the intestinal lumen and absorbed by duodenal mucosal cells [5]. Both retinyl esters and provitamin A carotenoids are converted to retinol, which is oxidized to retinal and then to retinoic acid [2]. Most of the body’s vitamin A is stored in the liver in the form of retinyl esters.
Retinol and carotenoid levels are typically measured in plasma, and plasma retinol levels are useful for assessing vitamin A inadequacy. However, their value for assessing marginal vitamin A status is limited because they do not decline until vitamin A levels in the liver are almost depleted [3]. Liver vitamin A reserves can be measured indirectly through the relative dose-response test, in which plasma retinol levels are measured before and after the administration of a small amount of vitamin A [5]. A plasma retinol level increase of at least 20% indicates an inadequate vitamin A level [3,5,6]. For clinical practice purposes, plasma retinol levels alone are sufficient for documenting significant deficiency.
A plasma retinol concentration lower than 0.70 micromoles/L (or 20 micrograms [mcg]/dL) reflects vitamin A inadequacy in a population, and concentrations of 0.70–1.05 micromoles/L could be marginal in some people [5]. In some studies, high plasma or serum concentrations of some provitamin A carotenoids have been associated with a lower risk of various health outcomes, but these studies have not definitively demonstrated that this relationship is causal.
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks:
Acute and chronic toxicity syndromes can both occur since Vitamin A is fat soluble and can build up in the body. Elevated intracranial pressure and decreased bone density are the most common concerns with excess Vitamin A. Although excess preformed vitamin A can have significant toxicity (known as hypervitaminosis A), large amounts of beta-carotene and other provitamin A carotenoids are not associated with major adverse effects [9]. The manifestations of hypervitaminosis A depend on the size and rapidity of the excess intake. The symptoms of hypervitaminosis A following sudden, massive intakes of vitamin A, as with Arctic explorers who ate polar bear liver, are acute [10]. Chronic intakes of excess vitamin A lead to increased intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri), dizziness, nausea, headaches, skin irritation, pain in joints and bones, coma, and even death [2,4,5]. Although hypervitaminosis A can be due to excessive dietary intakes, the condition is usually a result of consuming too much preformed vitamin A from supplements or therapeutic retinoids [3,5]. When people consume too much vitamin A, their tissue levels take a long time to fall after they discontinue 
The FNB has established ULs for preformed vitamin A that apply to both food and supplement intakes [5]. 
These ULs only apply to products from animal sources and supplements whose vitamin A comes entirely from retinol or its ester forms, such as retinyl palmitate. However, many dietary supplements (such as multivitamins) do not provide all of their vitamin A as retinol or its ester forms. For example, the vitamin A in some supplements consists partly or entirely of beta-carotene or other provitamin A carotenoids. In such cases, the percentage of retinol or retinyl ester in the supplement should be used to determine whether an individual’s vitamin A intake exceeds the UL. For example, a supplement whose label indicates that the product contains 3,000 mcg RAE vitamin A and that 60% of this vitamin A comes from beta-carotene (and therefore 40% comes from retinol or retinyl ester) provides 1,200 mcg RAE of preformed vitamin A. That amount is above the UL for children from birth to 8 years but below the UL for older children and adults.

clinical_details.introduction:
Vitamin K,” the generic name for a family of compounds with a common chemical structure of 2-methyl-1,4-naphthoquinone, is a fat-soluble vitamin that is naturally present in some foods and is available as a dietary supplement [1]. These compounds include phylloquinone (vitamin K1) and a series of menaquinones (vitamin K2) [2]. Menaquinones have unsaturated isoprenyl side chains and are designated as MK-4 through MK-13, based on the length of their side chain [1,2]. MK-4, MK-7, and MK-9 are the most well-studied menaquinones.

Phylloquinone is present primarily in green leafy vegetables and is the main dietary form of vitamin K [3]. Menaquinones, which are predominantly of bacterial origin, are present in modest amounts in various animal-based and fermented foods [1,4]. Almost all menaquinones, in particular the long-chain menaquinones, are also produced by bacteria in the human gut [5,6]. MK-4 is unique in that it is produced by the body from phylloquinone via a conversion process that does not involve bacterial action [7].

Vitamin K functions as a coenzyme for vitamin K-dependent carboxylase, an enzyme required for the synthesis of proteins involved in hemostasis (blood clotting) and bone metabolism, and other diverse physiological functions [3,5]. Prothrombin (clotting factor II) is a vitamin K-dependent protein in plasma that is directly involved in blood clotting. Warfarin (Coumadin®) and some anticoagulants used primarily in Europe antagonize the activity of vitamin K and, in turn, prothrombin [8]. For this reason, individuals who are taking these anticoagulants need to maintain consistent vitamin K intakes.

Matrix Gla-protein, a vitamin K-dependent protein present in vascular smooth muscle, bone, and cartilage, is the focus of considerable scientific research because it might help reduce abnormal calcification [9]. Osteocalcin is another vitamin K-dependent protein that is present in bone and may be involved in bone mineralization or turnover [5].
Like dietary lipids and other fat-soluble vitamins, ingested vitamin K is incorporated into mixed micelles via the action of bile and pancreatic enzymes, and it is absorbed by enterocytes of the small intestine [10]. From there, vitamin K is incorporated into chylomicrons, secreted into the lymphatic capillaries, transported to the liver, and repackaged into very low-density lipoproteins [2,10]. Vitamin K is present in the liver and other body tissues, including the brain, heart, pancreas, and bone [2,3,11].

In the circulation, vitamin K is carried mainly in lipoproteins [2]. Compared to the other fat-soluble vitamins, very small amounts of vitamin K circulate in the blood. Vitamin K is rapidly metabolized and excreted. Based on phylloquinone measurements, the body retains only about 30% to 40% of an oral physiological dose, while about 20% is excreted in the urine and 40% to 50% in the feces via bile [2,11]. This rapid metabolism accounts for vitamin K’s relatively low blood levels and tissue stores compared to those of the other fat-soluble vitamins [11].

Little is known about the absorption and transport of vitamin K produced by gut bacteria, but research indicates that substantial quantities of long-chain menaquinones are present in the large bowel [7]. Although the amount of vitamin K that the body obtains in this manner is unclear, experts believe that these menaquinones satisfy at least some of the body’s requirement for vitamin K [6,7].

In most cases, vitamin K status is not routinely assessed, except in individuals who take anticoagulants or have bleeding disorders. The only clinically significant indicator of vitamin K status is prothrombin time (the time it takes for blood to clot), and ordinary changes in vitamin K intakes have rarely been shown to alter prothrombin time [5]. In healthy people, fasting concentrations of phylloquinone in plasma have been reported to range from 0.29 to 2.64 nmol/L [12]. However, it is not clear whether this measure can be used to quantitatively assess vitamin K status. People with plasma phylloquinone concentrations slightly below the normal range have no clinical indications of vitamin K deficiency, possibly because plasma phylloquinone concentrations do not measure the contribution of menaquinones from the diet and the large bowel [12]. No data on normal ranges of menaquinones are available [2].
clinical_details.side_effects_and_risks: The main side effects appear to be potential interaction with medications which has been better studied with Vitamin K2 but could also potentially be a factor with Vitamin K2.
